title: Cat Sneezing Blood: Causes, Urgency, and Next Steps
meta_desc: If your cat is sneezing with bloody discharge, learn about the potential causes and why this medium-urgency condition requires a veterinary exam.
content_html:
TL;DR: Bloody sneezing in cats usually requires a veterinary appointment within 24 to 48 hours, but profuse bleeding or difficulty breathing are signs of a life-threatening emergency.

What causes bloody sneezing in my cat and what does it mean?

Frequent sneezing accompanied by bloody discharge, medically known as epistaxis, occurs when the delicate blood vessels in your cat's nasal passages are damaged. This can be caused by various factors including severe upper respiratory infections, nasal polyps, foreign bodies like grass seeds, or underlying issues such as dental disease or nasal tumors.

When is bloody sneezing in my cat considered a veterinary emergency?

  • The urgency level for this symptom is generally considered Medium.
  • Schedule a veterinary appointment within 24 to 48 hours if your cat is otherwise acting normally.
  • Seek emergency care immediately if the bleeding is profuse and constant.
  • Go to an emergency clinic if your cat is lethargic or having difficulty breathing.

How can taking a photo of my cat’s bloody discharge help the veterinarian?

  • Capture a clear photo of the discharge or your cat's nasal area to provide visual evidence for your veterinarian.
  • A photo allows the vet to assess the volume, color, and consistency of the blood without waiting for a sneezing episode to occur in the clinic.
  • This documentation helps the medical team differentiate between an acute injury and a chronic inflammatory process.

Clinical Context (Merck Veterinary Manual)

Causes of sneezing with blood (epistaxis or serosanguineous discharge) in cats include oronasal neoplasia, dental disease with oronasal fistula, nasal foreign body, and nasopharyngeal disease, such as a foreign body or mass lesion. Diagnostic steps include a thorough history and physical examination, complete blood count, serum chemistry profile, urinalysis, one-stage prothrombin time, partial thromboplastin time, and buccal mucosal bleeding time in addition to a platelet count to exclude systemic disease. Imaging of the nasal chambers via nasal or dental radiographs, or preferably CT, should be considered. Rhinoscopy, retroflex nasopharyngoscopy, nasal flushing, and nasal biopsy are useful diagnostic tools but should be delayed until after imaging to avoid iatrogenic changes. Chronic nasal discharge that changes in character from mucopurulent to serosanguineous or hemorrhagic suggests neoplastic or mycotic disease.

Chapter: Otorhinolaryngology, Diagnostic Imaging, Clinical Pathology

Source: The Merck Veterinary Manual, 11th Edition (Page 1480)

faqs.question: What are common causes of blood when a cat sneezes?
faqs.answer: Common causes include feline viral respiratory infections, fungal infections, nasal polyps, foreign objects stuck in the nose, or high blood pressure.

faqs.question: Should I try to clean my cat's nose?
faqs.answer: You can gently wipe away visible discharge with a soft, damp cloth, but do not insert anything into the nostrils, as this can cause further irritation or bleeding.

faqs.question: Will bloody sneezing go away on its own?
faqs.answer: It is unlikely to resolve without treatment if the cause is an infection, a foreign body, or a growth. A professional diagnosis is necessary to ensure the correct treatment.
